text:
This paper presents a robust and accurate method for text detection in natural scene images, utilizing Maximally Stable Extremal Regions (MSERs) as fundamental character candidates. To manage the high false-positive rate inherent in MSER extraction, we develop a fast pruning algorithm that employs a strategy of minimizing regularized variations to isolate candidate characters effectively. These candidates are subsequently grouped into potential text lines using a single-link clustering algorithm. A key contribution of our work is a novel self-training distance metric learning algorithm that automatically determines optimal distance weights and clustering thresholds. To refine these results, we estimate the posterior probabilities of text candidates being non-text via a character classifier; candidates with high non-text probabilities are eliminated, and the remaining regions are verified by a final text classifier. 

The proposed system was evaluated on the ICDAR 2011 Robust Reading Competition dataset, achieving an f-measure of 76%, which significantly exceeds the previous state-of-the-art of 71%. Further testing on a public multilingual dataset demonstrated the method’s versatility and robustness, showing a 9% improvement in f-measure over existing competitive techniques. Our results indicate that the integration of automated metric learning and hierarchical pruning offers a superior framework for handling the complexities of font, scale, and background in natural scene text detection.
